item.title: Re: Brilliant.
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much! I’m sorry that the necromancy bit is still hard to catch, but I’ll try my best to make it clearer.

I’m glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: Brilliant.
item.content:encoded:
This made me so hype I legit needed a minute to collect my thoughts. I'm a sucker for computer SCPs in general, but they don't normally give me goosebumps. This one did.

Spoilers!
!sreliopS

I really like the AI's personality, it feels a couple steps removed from a normal human being, which is a great way to do an AI. The portrayal of an adversarial neural net as siblings fighting is just beautiful, and the final twist made me want to re-read the article to get all the hints.

The one thing I didn't catch at all is the necromancy, although it was rather clear when you pointed it out. I would suggest showing that the people who were saved by the AI remain connected to it somehow, like mentioning that the app can be deleted, but nobody ever does that.

The way you talk about cryptocurrencies is a nice touch. I like the idea that even for something that anti-authoritarian and decentralised, the Foundation can just go "yeah, just make a bubble, pop it in ██ years, sweep the remains in a box and put in on a shelf".

Overall, a great article and a definite +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks Nine! I’m glad you liked it!

I might just end up removing that part, considering a lot of people don’t seem to think it’s necessary.

In any case, thanks for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a large improvement over the draft I'd read previously. The Level 4 access required stuff didn't feel incredibly significant to warrant that classification, but I enjoyed the rest, especially with the twist on what was going on.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cannot think of a better way to do that. Re-reading it, it seems obvious. Maybe I'm just obtuse. :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you enjoy it! But I didn't make the MTF, I just didn't link it yet in case the skip flopped. It's linked now if you want to see more skips managed by Kappa-10!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotta say, I really like the task force being named "Skynet."

spoilers!
don't blame me

I don't know if I'm just dumb but I didn't pick up on the theme of necromancy until I looked at the last transcript another time. Pretty freaky overall, especially when the hotline says "Oh, I just programmed a few AIs to feel suicidal."

One thing is, if the hotline infiltrated the Foundation's systems, then it might be able to develop a counter to the containment procedure. Then again it's a Keter so that wouldn't be so surprising. Pretty good skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the comments. I’ll make some quick fixes and see where things go from there.

Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
nitpicks:

+ show block
– hide block

A human subject is in the process of committing suicide.

Should maybe be 'attempting'

and attempt to coerce the subject from committing suicide.

'dissuade' is much more fitting than 'coerce'

100% of subjects who respond to SCP-3323's phone call survive, regardless of means of self-termination.

I would find a better way to phrase this. As-is it appears that part of the anomalous effect is magic suicide-prevention abilities (as in, the way it's worded makes it read like it can, say, stop one from dying even if they follow through with the attempt), as opposed to GM just being incredibly convincing in talking people down.

I probably have more comments, but I need to cut this short.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I’m super glad that you enjoyed it!

+ show block
– hide block

I would prefer if more people could catch that it’s necromancy without having to look at the chat. What do you think would make it easier to understand?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Interesting read! I enjoyed the "twist" that the speaker was an AI.

+ Show
- Hide

I definitely got that the "dead" AI was the sister he mentioned earlier in the skip. I did not pick up on the necromancy piece until you mentioned it, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m sorry that you didn’t think it was decidedly spooky enough, but I’m at least glad you thought it had its moments.

I’ll try to explain a few confusing details

1. The servers can only be kept up so long as bills are paid, and cryptocurrency is the hardest form of currency to track. Since there’s no concrete location discovered, the only containment procedures are to effectively starve it out economically. If it has no means of funding, it can’t run.

2. It wasn’t a focus more than it was just a detail. They literally just said “I just wanted a cool acronym”.

3. Yes, it’s expected that there are AIs considering the big reveal. Though in reality, it’s just two.

+ show block
– hide block

He’s basically getting suicidal people to trust him so he can eventually sacrifice him in an attempt to use necromancy to bring his sister, the death AI, back to “life”.

That which was mentioned in step 4 I feel requires a bit of protection, considering how wide-spread this app is.

I hope this explains things better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PoI-668 will then introduce himself, and attempt to coerce the subject from committing suicide.

Totally read that as "subject into committing," which made it much more sinister. This was helped by the fact that "coerce" has an implication of force or threats, which made it sound like the hotline bullied people into not killing themselves.

As for the article itself, it feels like it's all over the place. There's cryptocurrency, which is played up as this big thing but turns out to be a red herring. There's a focus on the acronym for no particular reason. There are AI's.

Then for the big reveal:

Spoilers
This was my takeaway, at least

It turns out the dude is an AI, and his sister is the death AI, and he will "do anything if it'll bring her back."


How this process relates to what is going on in the article was unclear to me.

Moreover, for all of the dodecatuple-secret-clearance stuff on the last collapsible, it most just felt kinda meh. It's not a "oh shit" moment where disparate parts of the article snap into place. It doesn't force me to reevaluate what I just read. It's mostly just a "huh, okay" moment.

I did like the fact that, although the tropes you used for this (spooky computers, friendly dude chatting) are indicative of how typical spooky articles tend to get written up, the article itself is decidedly not spooky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I'm glad you liked it, even in a heartbreaking way.

It's always good to get approval from respected writers like you, Taff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The mental image of a neural network optimising ways to kill itself and its adversary seeking to avenge it by setting up an anomalous suicide panopticon is perhaps the most heartbreakingly SCP thing I've read on the site. First Lazar's human missiles, now this. Fuck.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to A Random Day, GreenWolf, HexaDon, Weryllium, Shaggydredlocks, 9Volt, DarkStuff, taylor_itkin, Modern_Erasmus, Boogey_Man23, DrChandra, and of course, Randomini for giving me a LOT of great ideas!

If I've forgotten you, please message me so I can add you to the list.

I hope you enjoy my skip about the Ultimate Suicide Hotline!

Image Background
